name: BenevolentAI
funding: $500M+ (peaked at >£1B IPO valuation via SPAC-like Euronext listing)
summary: UK AI drug discovery pioneer that combined a knowledge graph with machine learning to identify novel drug targets. Its lead program BEN-2293, a topical Pan-Trk inhibitor for atopic dermatitis, failed to hit statistically significant endpoints in a Phase IIa trial in April 2023. The company lost over 90% of its share value, laid off 180 staff in 2023 and another 30% (closing its US office) in April 2024, and delisted from Euronext Amsterdam via a merger with Osaka Holdings in March 2025, becoming a private 'techbio' company.
lesson: A powerful AI discovery engine does not de-risk clinical biology; investors and founders conflated 'AI-generated' with 'AI-validated,' and a single Phase II miss erased most of the company's public-market value.

name: Exscientia
funding: $500M+ raised before Nasdaq listing; acquired for ~$775M in stock
summary: Oxford-spinout AI drug design company that went public on Nasdaq in 2021, claiming its 'Centaur Chemist' platform could design molecules in a fraction of the usual time. Despite multiple pharma partnerships, its stock collapsed and it was acquired by fellow struggling AI-drug-discovery firm Recursion Pharmaceuticals in an all-stock deal (approved November 2024), ending its run as an independent public company.
lesson: Being first to IPO on the 'AI drug discovery' narrative did not translate into a durable standalone business; without clinical validation, two struggling platform companies merging is often a managed decline rather than a turnaround.

name: Atomwise
summary: Early AI drug-discovery pioneer that used deep convolutional neural networks (AtomNet) to predict small-molecule binding, signing 750+ partnerships with academics, biotechs and pharma. Despite this breadth, none of those partnerships advanced a molecule into human clinical testing over a decade of operation. The company cut about 30% of its workforce in December 2023, and founding CEO Abraham Heifets departed in 2024 after 12 years, along with other senior leaders.
lesson: A breadth-over-depth licensing strategy (hundreds of shallow partnerships) generated headlines but not a single clinical asset, showing that partnership count is a vanity metric unless it converts to pipeline progress.

name: Verge Genomics
summary: AI-driven neuroscience drug discovery company whose ALS candidate VRG50635 failed to demonstrate patient benefit in an early clinical trial in late 2024. The company laid off roughly 90% of its staff and rebranded as Verge Labs, abandoning clinical drug development to instead sell target-identification data to other drug developers.
lesson: AI-prioritized targets still have to survive human biology; when the flagship clinical trial fails, a platform company with no approved backup plan can lose nearly its entire workforce overnight.

name: Absci
funding: IPO'd 2021 at ~$2B valuation, raising hundreds of millions
summary: Public (Nasdaq: ABSI) AI-driven protein/antibody design company combining generative AI with wet-lab synthetic biology. Its stock fell more than 80% from its 2021 IPO peak, and the company conducted repeated rounds of layoffs (2022, 2023, and 2024), shrinking its market cap to under $400M and drawing employee criticism of 'ineffective executive management' and unclear path to profitability.
lesson: A striking generative-AI protein design demo does not guarantee investor patience; repeated layoffs after an IPO signal that the platform's revenue model lagged badly behind its funding narrative.

name: Owkin
summary: French-American AI biomedical research unicorn built on federated learning across hospital data for biomarker and drug-target discovery. In September 2025 the company announced cuts of 40-50 of its 325 roles as it pivoted its business model toward an agentic AI 'copilot' (Owkin K) and away from long-term pharma R&D contracts, with insiders citing repeated reorganizations and aggressive over-hiring after fundraising.
lesson: Unicorn valuations built on federated-learning research contracts can mask a fragile revenue model; hiring ahead of proven recurring revenue forces painful pivots when the core contract pipeline doesn't scale.

name: IBM Watson Health (Watson for Drug Discovery)
funding: ~$4B in IBM acquisitions folded into the unit
summary: IBM's flagship attempt to apply Watson AI to scientific/medical research, including a dedicated 'Watson for Drug Discovery' product meant to mine gene-drug-disease relationships. IBM halted sales of the drug-discovery tool in 2019 citing disappointing results, and in January 2022 sold the core Watson Health data and analytics assets to private equity firm Francisco Partners for roughly $1 billion — a fraction of the capital IBM had invested — with the assets rebranded as Merative.
lesson: Marketing an AI system as capable of transforming scientific discovery before the underlying models could reliably generalize to messy real-world biomedical data led to a costly, public retreat from an entire market category.

name: Insitro
summary: Machine-learning-native drug discovery company founded by former Coursera CEO Daphne Koller, using large-scale cellular data and ML models to identify drug targets and predict clinical outcomes. Despite strategic agreements with Eli Lilly and Gilead, insitro cut 22% of its workforce (about 65 people) in May 2025, with Koller calling it one of the hardest days of her career, citing a 'tumultuous' funding market and the need to extend runway into 2027.
lesson: Even a heavily credentialed founding team and strong pharma partnerships cannot fully insulate a capital-intensive ML-plus-wet-lab model from a prolonged biotech funding downturn.

name: Berg Health
funding: ~$400M invested in its AI platform and pipeline
summary: Early AI-driven biopharma pioneer (Berg LLC) that built a proprietary 'Interrogative Biology' AI platform to mine patient biological data for disease drivers, applied to oncology and diagnostics. After investing around $400 million over more than a decade without a marketed product, the company sold its AI platform and drug/diagnostic pipelines to an investor consortium (later branded BPGbio) in February 2023, effectively ending Berg as an independent entity.
lesson: A decade-plus and hundreds of millions of dollars spent building a proprietary biological AI platform is not sufficient without a commercialized product; distressed asset sales are the typical endgame for capital-intensive science platforms that never reach market.

name: Cyclica
summary: Toronto-based AI drug discovery company whose platform screened proteome-wide drug-target interactions for pharma clients. After roughly a decade of operating independently, Cyclica was acquired by Recursion Pharmaceuticals in May 2023 for just $40 million — a distressed-scale outcome relative to the years of capital and effort invested, with about 60 employees absorbed into Recursion.
lesson: A technically credible AI drug-discovery platform can still end in a bargain-basement acquisition if it never builds an independent, defensible revenue stream large enough to justify continued standalone funding.

name: Aria Pharmaceuticals (formerly twoXAR)
summary: Palo Alto AI drug discovery company (originally twoXAR) that used machine learning to predict drug-disease matches and signed partnerships with pharma including Santen and Ono. It rebranded as Aria Pharmaceuticals in 2021 to pivot toward advancing its own pipeline of 18 therapeutic programs, but is now listed as 'out of business' by startup trackers, having gone quiet without a public wind-down announcement.
lesson: Pivoting from a fee-for-service AI partnership model to owning an internal drug pipeline dramatically raises capital requirements; without the funding to match the new strategy, the company simply faded rather than scaling.

name: Verseon
summary: One of the earliest AI-driven, physics-based drug discovery companies, running de novo molecular design programs across cardiovascular, diabetes, cancer and liver disease. More than two decades after founding, Verseon remains a private company with no approved drug; its most advanced candidate (a thrombin inhibitor) was still in Phase 1 as of the early 2020s, an unusually long, unresolved development timeline for a company built around accelerating discovery.
lesson: A drug-discovery AI platform's core promise is speed; two decades without a single approval undercuts that value proposition regardless of how sophisticated the underlying molecular-physics models are.

name: 54gene
summary: Nigerian genomics company aiming to build Africa's largest genomic database to correct the underrepresentation of African genetic data in global pharmaceutical AI/drug-discovery research. Despite $45 million raised and high-profile recognition, the company burned through three CEOs in a year, could not sustain operations financially, and began winding down in July 2023, fully ceasing operations by September 2023.
lesson: A scientifically important and differentiated data asset (African genomic diversity) is not enough to survive leadership churn and an inability to convert that asset into recurring commercial revenue.

name: Entelos
summary: One of the pioneering computational/systems biology companies, building 'virtual patient' disease simulation models (PhysioLab) used by pharma to predict drug responses before clinical trials — an early precursor to today's AI-for-biology platforms. Entelos filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2011 and its assets were auctioned off; its consulting business was later acquired by Rosa & Co. in 2014.
lesson: Computational disease-simulation platforms were commercially ahead of both the compute and data availability needed to make them broadly useful — a cautionary precedent for today's AI-biology 'digital twin' startups riding on much better infrastructure.

name: Selventa (formerly Genstruct)
summary: Cambridge, MA computational systems biology company (founded as Genstruct, rebranded Selventa in 2010) that built a causal-reasoning platform and the widely-used Biological Expression Language (BEL) to model disease mechanisms for pharma partners including Pfizer. Despite technical influence on the field, Selventa ceased operations in 2016 after 15 years without achieving a self-sustaining commercial model.
lesson: A company can leave a lasting scientific/technical legacy (BEL is still used in computational biology today) while still failing commercially — technical influence and business viability are not the same thing.

name: Gro Intelligence
summary: AI/data platform aggregating agricultural, weather, geological and commodities data to model global food and climate systems, named one of TIME's most influential companies. After a decade and over $125M raised, the company could not make payroll by February 2024, its CEO departed, it laid off 60% of staff, and it fully shut down by June 2024 amid an SEC investigation and employee lawsuits over labor law violations.
lesson: Selling data-derived climate/agriculture insight is a hard, low-margin consulting-like business unless it converts into scalable recurring software revenue; over-reliance on a handful of large clients left the company with no cushion when relationships soured.

name: Zymergen
summary: Synthetic biology company that used machine learning and automated 'foundries' to design novel materials and molecules for industrial applications. Months after a $575M IPO in April 2021 at a ~$3B valuation, Zymergen admitted it would generate no meaningful revenue in 2021 or 2022 due to undisclosed technical problems, crashing its stock 68% in a single day. It was acquired by Ginkgo Bioworks in 2022 for just $300 million (one-tenth its IPO valuation), filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023, and liquidated in 2024; the SEC separately fined the company $30M for misleading IPO disclosures.
lesson: Overselling AI/automation-driven materials discovery capabilities to public investors ahead of proven commercial output is not just a business risk but a securities-fraud risk — the gap between the pitch and the lab reality became a regulatory matter, not just a market one.

name: Indigo Ag
summary: Agricultural biology company applying microbiome science and AI/data analytics to develop crop-boosting microbial treatments and a carbon-credit marketplace. After peaking at a $3.5B valuation, the company underwent three rounds of layoffs (2021, 2023, 2024), struggled to commercialize its microbiome products and grain marketplace against incumbents like Cargill and ADM, and its valuation reportedly cratered by roughly 94% in a subsequent funding round before it split into separate business units to survive.
lesson: Applying rigorous science and AI to a real problem (soil/plant microbiome) does not overcome go-to-market disadvantages against entrenched, low-margin agribusiness incumbents with decades of grower relationships.

name: Recursion Pharmaceuticals
summary: Public (Nasdaq: RXRX) AI-driven drug discovery company using automated cell-imaging and machine learning to map biology at scale, once promising to advance 100 drug programs within a decade — a target its own CEO later acknowledged would be missed. The company laid off 20% of staff in mid-2024, acquired similarly struggling Exscientia in November 2024 (triggering further layoffs and pipeline cuts), and cut another round of staff in 2025 to extend cash runway into 2027, with its stock down roughly 30%+ over trailing twelve months.
lesson: Ambitious, quantified public promises ('100 drugs in 10 years') set a bar that AI-native drug discovery, still bound by the slow, expensive realities of clinical trials, has not yet been able to clear — and missing that bar publicly erodes investor trust even for a well-capitalized platform leader.

name: C4X Discovery
funding: Publicly listed on AIM; £30M+ market cap pre-delisting
summary: UK AI/bioinformatics-driven small-molecule drug discovery company targeting immuno-inflammatory disease, publicly listed on London's AIM market. Citing a persistently languishing share price and the high regulatory cost of a public listing relative to the capital it could raise, C4X Discovery delisted from AIM in 2024 to pursue private venture funding instead — a tacit admission that public markets were not valuing the AI-drug-discovery story.
lesson: A sub-£30M market cap after over a decade as a public AI drug-discovery company shows that small-cap public investors lost patience with the category well before any clinical payoff, forcing a retreat to private markets.

name: e-Therapeutics
funding: Publicly listed on AIM; raised £28.9M in 2024 delisting round
summary: UK computational-biology company using network-pharmacology AI to discover RNAi medicines, publicly listed on London's AIM market for over two decades. In April 2024 the company announced it would quit AIM entirely, citing a 'lack of institutional UK interest' and 'risk appetite' for its story, ceasing trading in May 2024 and exploring a future Nasdaq listing instead.
lesson: More than twenty years as a public AI-driven drug discovery company without a marketed product exhausted the patience of UK public investors specifically, illustrating how geography and market sentiment — not just company execution — can end a public listing.

name: Ginkgo Bioworks
summary: Synthetic biology 'foundry' company combining automated lab robotics with AI/ML to design organisms and molecules for pharma, agriculture and industrial customers, and a serial acquirer of distressed AI-science peers (Zymergen assets, Reverie Labs). After going public via SPAC at roughly $15-17.5B, its stock fell more than 95% from peak, revenue guidance was slashed (a projected $628M cell-engineering business shrank to ~$175M), and the company cut at least 35% of its workforce (400+ jobs) in 2024 amid roughly $383M in first-half losses, later pivoting toward selling AI-enabled lab robots.
lesson: A platform-as-a-service model for synthetic biology (charging customers for foundry access) proved far less scalable and lucrative than SPAC-era projections assumed, and being a buyer of other distressed AI-science startups did not insulate the acquirer from the same underlying market problem.

name: Berkeley Lights / PhenomeX
summary: AI/automation-enabled single-cell biology instrument company (later merged with IsoPlexis and renamed PhenomeX) that IPO'd in 2020 at $22/share, peaking near $103 in late 2020. A class-action lawsuit over allegedly misleading statements about its technology's capabilities and performance, combined with slowing growth, drove the stock down to $0.40/share by mid-2023. Bruker acquired PhenomeX for just $1.00/share (~$108M total) in an all-cash deal that closed in October 2023 — a roughly 95% decline from IPO value.
lesson: Investor lawsuits over overstated technology performance claims are a recurring failure pattern among AI/automation-driven life-science instrument companies that go public before their platform's real-world capability matches the pitch.

name: Isomorphic Labs
summary: Google DeepMind spinoff built on the AlphaFold breakthrough, applying AI to design drugs by predicting protein structure and molecular interactions with unprecedented accuracy. Backed by Thrive Capital, Alphabet/GV, MGX, Temasek and others, it has signed major partnerships with Eli Lilly and Novartis, grown past 200 employees, and by mid-2026 was preparing to dose its first patients in human clinical trials.
lesson: Building on a genuinely field-defining scientific breakthrough (AlphaFold) combined with elite technical talent and patient capital lets an AI-for-science company command billion-dollar rounds years before any product revenue — but the real test (clinical success) still lies ahead.

name: EvolutionaryScale
funding: $142M seed round; acquired by Chan Zuckerberg Biohub
summary: AI biotechnology company founded by former Meta FAIR researchers who built the ESM family of protein language models. Its ESM3 model, trained on 2.78 billion protein sequences, generated a novel fluorescent protein that would have taken hundreds of millions of years to evolve naturally. Backed by Amazon and Nvidia among others at launch, the company was acquired by the nonprofit Chan Zuckerberg Biohub in 2025 to accelerate open, AI-powered biological research.
lesson: A scientifically landmark open-source-adjacent foundation model (ESM3) can command a premium acquisition even without a conventional commercial drug pipeline, if it demonstrably advances the state of the art in a foundational science domain.

name: Xaira Therapeutics
summary: AI drug discovery company launched with over $1 billion in committed funding, one of the largest biotech launches in history, co-founded by Nobel laureate David Baker (protein design) and led by former Genentech CSO Marc Tessier-Lavigne. Backed by Arch Venture Partners, Foresite Labs, Sequoia, NEA and others, Xaira builds on protein/antibody design breakthroughs (RFdiffusion, RFantibody) from Baker's lab and has since unveiled X-Cell, an AI model for predicting gene function.
lesson: Marrying a Nobel Prize-caliber scientific advantage (Baker's protein design lab) with experienced pharma operators let Xaira raise at a scale ($1B+) that gives it years of runway to prove out AI-designed therapeutics before facing market pressure.

name: Iambic Therapeutics
summary: AI-driven drug discovery company whose generative models (Enchant, NeuralPLexer for protein-ligand structure prediction) power both internal cancer-drug programs and partner collaborations. It raised over $100M in an oversubscribed round in late 2025 following encouraging clinical data for an AI-discovered cancer drug, and signed a partnership with Takeda potentially worth more than $1.7 billion, alongside earlier deals with Revolution Medicines.
lesson: Publishing real clinical data from an AI-discovered molecule — not just discovery-speed claims — is what unlocked both oversubscribed fundraising and a billion-dollar-plus pharma partnership.

name: Periodic Labs
funding: $300M seed, reportedly raising further at $7.5B valuation
summary: Materials science AI startup founded by former OpenAI VP of Research Liam Fedus and ex-DeepMind scientist Ekin Dogus Cubuk, building autonomous robotic laboratories that run physics and chemistry experiments to generate proprietary training data for discovering new materials, including next-generation superconductors. It raised a $300M seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz within two months of founding, reportedly valuing it at up to $1.5B, with talks underway for a further $500M round at a $7.5B valuation.
lesson: Elite AI-lab pedigree plus a credible 'self-driving lab' data-generation strategy for materials discovery can command unprecedented seed-stage valuations, reflecting investor appetite for AI applied to hard physical sciences beyond drug discovery.

name: Chemify
summary: Scottish AI-and-robotics chemistry company founded by Professor Lee Cronin, building on two decades of University of Glasgow research to digitize molecule creation via 'Chemputation' — a programming language (χDL) and machine learning system that translates molecular design into automated robotic synthesis. It raised an oversubscribed $50M Series B in 2025 led by Wing Venture Capital and Insight Partners to expand global 'digital chemistry' operations.
lesson: Combining a genuinely novel scientific IP base (two decades of academic robotic-chemistry research) with a clear automation value proposition let Chemify raise successive oversubscribed rounds rather than struggle to justify its thesis.

name: Terray Therapeutics
summary: AI-powered small-molecule drug discovery company that built what it says is the world's largest chemistry dataset (5B+ target-ligand interactions) and published its COATI chemistry foundation model. It closed an oversubscribed $120M Series B in 2024 led by Bedford Ridge Capital with Nvidia's NVentures participating, bringing total funding past $200M to advance an AI-generated pipeline toward the clinic.
lesson: Investing early in proprietary, massive-scale experimental datasets (rather than relying solely on public chemistry data) gave Terray a defensible data moat that attracted strategic investors like Nvidia.

name: Generate Biomedicines
summary: Flagship Pioneering-incubated 'generative biology' company that programs protein-based therapeutic modalities directly from AI models rather than screening existing molecules. Its platform underpins a partnership with Amgen worth up to $1.9 billion, and the company has continued to expand its generative protein design pipeline across multiple therapeutic modalities.
lesson: Treating protein design as a generative-AI problem (create novel proteins to spec) rather than a screening problem (search existing libraries) opened a large-scale pharma partnership most traditional discovery platforms couldn't match.

name: Cradle Bio
summary: Amsterdam/Zurich-based AI protein engineering software company that raised a $73M Series B led by IVP in 2025 to expand its generative-AI protein design platform, which reduces the number of experimental rounds needed to engineer better proteins. It grew from two paying customers at Series A to 21 customers including four of the top ten global pharma companies (Johnson & Johnson, Novo Nordisk among them).
lesson: A software-first (rather than wet-lab-heavy) approach to protein engineering allowed Cradle to scale customer count 10x between funding rounds with comparatively capital-efficient growth.

name: Enveda Biosciences
summary: AI-driven natural-product drug discovery company that uses machine learning and mass spectrometry (its PRISM platform) to analyze thousands of plant-derived molecules simultaneously, sidestepping slow traditional NMR analysis. It has raised over $400M across multiple rounds, most recently a $150M Series D led by Premji Invest, to advance a pipeline of ten AI-discovered drug candidates targeting immunology, obesity and neurological disorders toward 2025-2026 clinical readouts.
lesson: Applying AI to an underexploited data source (natural/plant-derived chemical diversity) rather than competing head-on in synthetic small-molecule space gave Enveda a differentiated thesis that sustained large, consecutive funding rounds.

name: Aqemia
summary: Paris-based drug discovery company combining generative AI with quantum-inspired statistical-mechanics algorithms to predict drug-target binding affinity roughly 10,000x faster than standard simulation software. It raised €60M in Series A funding and further rounds bringing total funding past $100M, and signed a partnership with Sanofi worth up to $140M to identify new drug candidates.
lesson: Combining a genuinely differentiated computational approach (quantum-inspired physics simulation, not just ML pattern-matching) gave Aqemia a defensible technical story that translated into a major pharma partnership.

name: Iktos
summary: Paris-based generative-AI and robotics company for medicinal chemistry, combining AI molecule design (Makya), retrosynthesis planning (Spaya) and automated robotic synthesis. It completed a €15.5M Series A in 2023, acquired imaging/screening company Synsight in 2024, secured a €2.5M European Innovation Council grant in 2025, and signed an integrated drug-discovery collaboration with Pierre Fabre Laboratories in oncology, with over 60 completed pharma projects to date.
lesson: Steady, moderate fundraising paired with continuous platform expansion (adding retrosynthesis and robotics to core generative design) let Iktos build a sustainable, partnership-driven business without needing mega-rounds.

name: Schrödinger, Inc.
summary: Publicly traded computational chemistry pioneer whose physics-based molecular simulation platform is used across both software licensing (to pharma and materials companies) and its own drug discovery pipeline. In fiscal 2025 the company reported total revenue of $255.9M, up 23.3% year-over-year, with software revenue reaching $199.5M and a strong $402.3M cash position, despite continuing net losses as it invests in pipeline expansion.
lesson: A dual software-licensing-plus-pipeline business model gives computational science companies a revenue floor (recurring software sales) that pure drug-discovery-only AI companies lack, providing more resilience through biotech funding cycles.

name: Numerate
funding: Series funding undisclosed; acquired by Valo Health
summary: Early machine-learning-based small-molecule drug discovery company that partnered with pharma including Lundbeck on neuroscience programs. Numerate was acquired by Valo Health in September 2020, with its technology and team folded into Valo's broader AI drug discovery platform — a successful strategic exit after over a decade of independent operation.
lesson: Not every successful outcome in AI-for-science is an IPO; a well-timed acquisition into a larger, better-capitalized platform can be the right exit for a technically strong but sub-scale AI discovery company.

name: Chematica / Grzybowski Scientific Inventions
summary: Developer of Chematica, a computer-aided synthesis planning system combining machine learning with expert chemical rules to identify optimal chemical synthesis routes, created by chemist Bartosz Grzybowski. Merck KGaA's MilliporeSigma division acquired the company (Grzybowski Scientific Inventions) in 2017, and the software was commercialized as Synthia, becoming a widely used retrosynthesis planning tool across the chemical industry.
lesson: A narrowly-scoped but genuinely useful AI application (synthesis route planning) built by a domain expert can achieve a clean strategic acquisition by a major industrial chemistry player years before the current AI-for-science funding wave.

name: Kayrros
summary: Paris-based environmental and energy intelligence company using AI, machine learning and satellite geoanalytics across 20+ satellite constellations to turn raw Earth-observation data into insights on emissions, supply chains and physical climate risk. Energy analytics firm Energy Aspects completed its acquisition of Kayrros in 2026, combining satellite-derived environmental intelligence with Energy Aspects' market research.
lesson: Satellite-and-AI-driven environmental intelligence companies can find durable exits by aligning with adjacent data/analytics incumbents (energy market intelligence) who need geospatial climate data as a strategic complement, not a standalone product.

name: Sust Global
summary: California-based climate risk intelligence company using machine learning and satellite data to help institutional investors assess physical climate risk across investment portfolios. ISS STOXX, the sustainable investment arm of ISS, acquired Sust Global to add geospatial AI-powered climate risk capabilities to its ESG and climate-risk investment analytics offerings.
lesson: A tightly-scoped climate-AI product built specifically for institutional investor workflows (rather than a broad climate-data platform) found a natural acquirer in an established investment-analytics incumbent.

name: Reverie Labs
funding: $25M+ raised; platform acquired by Ginkgo Bioworks
summary: AI drug discovery company developing brain-penetrant cancer therapies and large-scale AI foundation models for chemistry. In February 2024, Ginkgo Bioworks acquired Reverie's AI infrastructure and model-training software, with four of Reverie's key AI team members joining Ginkgo to integrate the technology into broader AI-driven biology engineering efforts.
lesson: A focused AI-infrastructure asset (foundation-model training tooling for chemistry) can be valuable enough on its own to justify an acquihire-plus-technology deal, even without a company-wide exit.

name: Nabla Bio
summary: AI protein/antibody design company whose JAM (Joint Atomic Modeling) platform generates antibodies against specified disease targets. Backed by Khosla Ventures, Zetta Venture Partners and Radical Ventures, Nabla has signed strategic collaborations with AstraZeneca, Bristol Myers Squibb and Takeda worth over $550 million in potential upfront and milestone payments plus royalties.
lesson: A capital-efficient seed-to-Series-A raise, focused tightly on antibody design rather than broad drug discovery, let Nabla stack multiple large pharma partnerships that dwarf its own funding total.

name: Latent Labs
summary: AI-programmable biology company founded by Dr. Simon Kohl, a former co-lead of DeepMind's protein design team and senior researcher on AlphaFold2, focused on democratizing protein design. It raised $50M total, including a $40M Series A co-led by Radical Ventures and Sofinnova Partners, and launched a web-based AI model for protein design in 2025, backed by high-profile angels including Google's Jeff Dean and Cohere's Aidan Gomez.
lesson: AlphaFold-lineage technical pedigree remains one of the strongest fundraising signals in AI-for-science, letting a founding team raise a substantial Series A within roughly a year of founding.

name: Tomorrow.io (formerly ClimaCell)
summary: Boston-based weather intelligence company combining proprietary satellite data with AI and real-time forecasting for enterprises and governments, rebranded from ClimaCell to Tomorrow.io in 2021. It raised $175M in a 2026 round at over a $1B valuation, achieving unicorn status, and announced a technical collaboration with Nvidia's Earth-2 platform to help build an AI-powered digital twin of Earth's climate system.
lesson: Building proprietary data infrastructure (its own satellite constellation) alongside AI forecasting models — rather than relying solely on public weather data — differentiated Tomorrow.io enough to reach unicorn status in a space with many free/cheap incumbents (NOAA, ECMWF).

name: XtalPi
summary: Tencent-backed Chinese AI drug discovery company combining artificial intelligence and quantum physics simulation for molecular discovery. XtalPi completed a $126.7M Hong Kong IPO in June 2024, becoming the first AI drug discovery company to go public in Hong Kong (beating rival Insilico Medicine to market), with shares surging as much as 24.6% on debut.
lesson: Being first-to-market with a public listing in a receptive exchange (Hong Kong, for Chinese AI-biotech) can itself be a competitive advantage, capturing investor attention ahead of larger but slower-to-IPO rivals.

name: Insilico Medicine
summary: AI-driven biotechnology company (target discovery, drug design and aging/longevity research) that hit unicorn status with a $110M round in early 2025, then listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in December 2025 in the largest biotech IPO in Hong Kong that year, becoming the first AI-driven biomedical company to list under HKEX's profit-test rule. It signed an R&D deal with French pharma Servier worth up to $888M.
lesson: Meeting a stricter 'profit test' listing bar (rather than a pre-revenue biotech listing route) signaled genuine commercial traction and helped Insilico command the largest Hong Kong biotech IPO of 2025.

name: Vividion Therapeutics
funding: Acquired by Bayer for up to $2B
summary: Chemoproteomics-driven drug discovery company founded by Scripps Research chemists, using a screening platform to find previously unknown drug-binding pockets ('undruggable' targets) across the human proteome. Bayer acquired Vividion in an arm's-length deal worth $1.5B upfront plus up to $500M in milestones (total up to $2B), lured away from a planned IPO, to bolster its own drug discovery R&D platform.
lesson: A scientifically differentiated chemistry platform (targeting previously 'undruggable' proteins) can command a premium strategic acquisition that beats even a well-timed IPO, when a big pharma buyer values platform control over public-market optionality.

name: Orbital Materials
summary: AI materials discovery company founded by ex-DeepMind researcher Jonathan Godwin, originally focused on AI-discovered carbon-capture sorbents and sustainable-aviation-fuel chemistry using its LINUS generative chemistry model. Facing weak near-term commercial pull for climate materials, the company (rebranded Orbital Industries) pivoted its AI-designed-materials expertise toward PFAS-free GPU cooling fluids and modular data-center infrastructure, raising a $50M Series B led by Plural with Nvidia's NVentures participating.
lesson: When a scientifically sound AI-for-climate-materials mission meets weak near-term commercial demand, redirecting the same core technology (generative materials AI) toward an adjacent market with urgent buyer demand (AI-datacenter infrastructure) can turn a stalling mission into a funded, growing business.
